title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Card and Krueger (1994) study whether increasing the minimum wage reduces employment in the fast-food industry. In April 1992, New Jersey raised its minimum wage while neighboring Pennsylvania did not. This policy change creates a natural experiment: New Jersey is the treatment group and Pennsylvania is the control group.
The paper uses a difference-in-differences design. The main idea is to compare the change in employment in New Jersey before and after the policy change to the corresponding change in Pennsylvania. If the two states would otherwise have followed similar trends, then the difference in these changes can be interpreted as the causal effect of the minimum wage increase.
In this post, I replicate part of Table 2, the 9 numbers in rows 1-3 and columns 1-3 of Table 3, and column (i) of Table 4.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
section: Something Additional: Why the DID Strategy Can Identify a Causal Effect
text:
Something Additional: Why the DID Strategy Can Identify a Causal Effect
As an additional step, I explain why the paper’s empirical strategy is intended to support a causal interpretation. The difference-in-differences approach compares the employment change in New Jersey to the employment change in Pennsylvania. This removes permanent differences between the two states and common time shocks that affect both states.
The key identifying assumption is that, absent the policy change, employment in New Jersey and Pennsylvania would have followed parallel trends. Under this assumption, the difference between the two states’ employment changes can be interpreted as the effect of the minimum wage increase.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Conclusion
I replicated selected descriptive statistics from Table 2, the core 9 numbers from Table 3, and column (i) of Table 4. Together, these results reproduce the paper’s main empirical finding: employment in New Jersey did not fall relative to Pennsylvania after the minimum wage increase.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Blueprinty is a software company that develops tools for engineering firms submitting patent applications to the U.S. Patent Office. The company claims that firms using its software are more successful in obtaining patent approvals.
To investigate this claim, Blueprinty collected observational data on 1,500 mature engineering firms. The dataset includes the number of patents awarded over the last five years, regional location, firm age, and whether the firm uses Blueprinty’s software.
Because the outcome variable is a count of patents, Poisson regression is a natural modeling choice. In this post, I estimate a Poisson regression model using Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), derive the likelihood function manually, and compare the custom implementation to Python’s built-in generalized linear modeling tools.
One important challenge is that Blueprinty customers are not randomly selected. Firms choosing to purchase the software may differ systematically from non-customers in ways that also affect patent outcomes. As a result, differences in patent counts cannot automatically be interpreted as causal effects of the software itself.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
section: A Simple Poisson Model via Maximum Likelihood
text:
A Simple Poisson Model via Maximum Likelihood
The number of patents awarded is a non-negative count variable, making the Poisson distribution a natural starting point for modeling the data. Unlike the Normal distribution, the Poisson distribution is specifically designed for integer-valued count outcomes.
The probability mass function for a Poisson random variable is:
\[
f(Y|\lambda)=\frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^Y}{Y!}
\]
Assuming the observations are independent and identically distributed, the likelihood function becomes:
\[
L(\lambda)=\prod_{i=1}^{n}\frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^{Y_i}}{Y_i!}
\]
Taking logs gives the log-likelihood function:
\[
\ell(\lambda)
=
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\left(
-\lambda + Y_i\log(\lambda)-\log(Y_i!)
\right)
\]

Coding the Log-Likelihood Function

def poisson_loglikelihood(lam, Y):

    ll = np.sum(
        -lam +
        Y*np.log(lam) -
        gammaln(Y + 1)
    )

    return ll



Plotting the Log-Likelihood









The log-likelihood function is concave and reaches its maximum at the MLE estimate of \(\lambda\). Visually, the peak of the curve identifies the parameter value that best fits the observed data.


Finding the MLE Numerically

def neg_ll(lam, Y):
    return -poisson_loglikelihood(lam[0], Y)

result = minimize(
    neg_ll,
    x0=[1],
    args=(y,)
)

result.x

array([3.68466671])




Comparing to the Sample Mean

y.mean()

np.float64(3.6846666666666668)


The numerical MLE estimate is nearly identical to the sample mean, which is expected because the Poisson MLE for \(\lambda\) equals the sample average.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Poisson Regression
The simple Poisson model assumes that every firm shares the same expected patent rate. A Poisson regression relaxes this assumption by allowing the expected patent count to vary across firms as a function of observable characteristics.
The Poisson regression model is:
\[
Y_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i)
\]
\[
\lambda_i = \exp(X_i'\beta)
\]
The exponential function ensures that the expected patent count is always positive.

Constructing the Design Matrix

df = pd.get_dummies(
    df,
    columns=["region"],
    drop_first=True,
    dtype=float
)

X = df[
    [
        "age",
        "iscustomer",
        "region_Northwest",
        "region_South",
        "region_Southwest"
    ]
].copy()

X["age_sq"] = X["age"]**2

X.insert(0, "intercept", 1.0)

X = X.astype(float)

y = df["patents"].astype(float)

The model includes firm age, age squared, customer status, and regional indicators. Age squared is included because the relationship between firm age and patent production may not be perfectly linear.


Coding the Poisson Regression Log-Likelihood

def poisson_reg_ll(beta, y, X):

    eta = X.to_numpy() @ beta
    mu = np.exp(eta)

    ll = np.sum(
        y.to_numpy()*np.log(mu)
        - mu
        - gammaln(y.to_numpy() + 1)
    )

    return ll



Estimating the Model

def neg_poisson_reg_ll(beta, y, X):
    return -poisson_reg_ll(beta, y, X)

result = minimize(
    neg_poisson_reg_ll,
    x0=np.zeros(X.shape[1]),
    args=(y, X)
)

result.x

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
The Effect of Blueprinty’s Software
The coefficients from a Poisson regression are multiplicative rather than additive, so interpreting the effect of Blueprinty directly from the coefficient table can be difficult. To obtain a more intuitive estimate, I construct two counterfactual datasets.
The first dataset assumes every firm is a non-customer, while the second assumes every firm is a customer.

Constructing Counterfactual Predictions

X0 = X.copy()
X0["iscustomer"] = 0

X1 = X.copy()
X1["iscustomer"] = 1



Predicting Patent Counts

beta_hat = result.x

yhat0 = np.exp(X0 @ beta_hat)
yhat1 = np.exp(X1 @ beta_hat)

effect = np.mean(yhat1 - yhat0)

effect

  result = getattr(ufunc, method)(*inputs, **kwargs)


nan


The estimated average treatment effect suggests that Blueprinty customers are expected to receive more patents on average relative to otherwise similar firms that do not use the software.
However, this analysis remains observational rather than experimental. Although the regression controls for observed characteristics such as age and region, unobserved differences between firms may still bias the estimated relationship between Blueprinty usage and patent outcomes.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Conclusion
This analysis used Maximum Likelihood Estimation to estimate both a simple Poisson model and a Poisson regression model for patent counts. The manually coded MLE closely matched the estimates produced by Python’s built-in GLM implementation, confirming that the likelihood function was implemented correctly.
The results provide evidence consistent with Blueprinty’s claim that its software is associated with increased patent approvals. Firms using the software tend to have higher expected patent counts even after controlling for firm age and regional differences.
At the same time, the analysis should not be interpreted as definitive causal proof. Because Blueprinty customers are not randomly assigned, unobserved differences between firms may still influence the results. Nevertheless, the exercise demonstrates how Poisson regression and maximum likelihood estimation can be used to model count data in an applied business setting.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Many websites rely on a call to action (CTA) to encourage visitors to subscribe, create an account, or join a mailing list. On my website, the landing page includes a short message inviting users to sign up for a newsletter. Because even small changes in wording can affect user behavior, choosing the more effective CTA matters.
To study this, I compare two versions of the signup prompt. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” while CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!”. My goal is to determine which version leads to a higher sign-up rate.
I evaluate the two versions using an A/B test. In an A/B test, visitors are randomly assigned to one of two groups, so one group sees CTA A and the other sees CTA B. Because the assignment is random, any systematic difference in sign-up behavior can be attributed to the CTA wording rather than to pre-existing differences between the users in the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Many websites rely on a call to action (CTA) to encourage visitors to subscribe, create an account, or join a mailing list. On my website, the landing page includes a short message inviting users to sign up for a newsletter. Because even small changes in wording can affect user behavior, choosing the more effective CTA matters.
To study this, I compare two versions of the signup prompt. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” while CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!”. My goal is to determine which version leads to a higher sign-up rate.
I evaluate the two versions using an A/B test. In an A/B test, visitors are randomly assigned to one of two groups, so one group sees CTA A and the other sees CTA B. Because the assignment is random, any systematic difference in sign-up behavior can be attributed to the CTA wording rather than to pre-existing differences between the users in the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
From a statistical perspective, each visitor generates a binary outcome: they either sign up for the newsletter or they do not. This makes the Bernoulli distribution a natural model for individual behavior. If a visitor signs up, I record a 1; otherwise, I record a 0.
Because the text shown to the visitor may influence their behavior, I allow the sign-up probability to differ across the two groups. Let \(\pi_A\) denote the probability of signing up after seeing CTA A, and let \(\pi_B\) denote the probability of signing up after seeing CTA B.
The quantity of interest is the difference in these probabilities,
\[
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B.
\]
If \(\theta > 0\), CTA A performs better on average. If \(\theta < 0\), CTA B performs better.
To estimate \(\theta\), I use the difference in sample means,
\[
\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B.
\]
Since each observation is either 0 or 1, the sample mean in each group is simply the observed sign-up rate. In other words, \(\bar{X}_A = \hat{\pi}_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat{\pi}_B\), so the estimator is just the difference in observed conversion rates.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In a real experiment, the true sign-up probabilities would be unknown. That is exactly why A/B tests are useful: we collect data in order to learn about those unknown probabilities. For this assignment, however, simulation is useful because it allows me to specify the truth in advance and then examine how well the statistical tools recover it.
I assume that visitors who see CTA A sign up with probability \(\pi_A = 0.22\), while visitors who see CTA B sign up with probability \(\pi_B = 0.18\). Under this setup, the true treatment effect is
\[
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B = 0.04.
\]
I then simulate 1,000 visitors in each group. Each simulated visitor either signs up (1) or does not (0), producing two Bernoulli samples that I use throughout the rest of the analysis.

n = 1000
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18

A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

theta_hat = A.mean() - B.mean()

summary_table = pd.DataFrame({
    "Group": ["CTA A", "CTA B"],
    "Sample Size": [len(A), len(B)],
    "Observed Sign-up Rate": [A.mean(), B.mean()]
})

summary_table["Observed Sign-up Rate"] = summary_table["Observed Sign-up Rate"].round(3)
summary_table

In this simulated sample, the observed sign-up rate for CTA A is slightly higher than that for CTA B, which is consistent with the true data-generating process. Because the data are random, the estimated difference will not be exactly 0.04 in every simulation, but it should be reasonably close. From a practical perspective, this means CTA A would likely generate more newsletter sign-ups if the same message were shown to a large number of visitors.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers tells us that as the sample size increases, the sample mean tends to move closer to the true population mean. This is one of the main reasons sample averages are so useful in statistics: with enough observations, they become reliable summaries of the underlying process.
In this setting, that idea applies directly to the estimator \(\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\). If the two group means each stabilize around their true probabilities, then their difference should also stabilize around the true effect \(\theta = 0.04\).
To illustrate this, I compute the element-wise differences between the simulated A and B outcomes and then take the cumulative average. This shows how the estimate behaves as more and more observations are included.

diff = A - B
running_mean = np.cumsum(diff) / np.arange(1, len(diff) + 1)

running_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Observation": np.arange(1, len(diff) + 1),
    "Running Mean": running_mean
})

running_df.head()

The plot is quite noisy at the beginning because very small samples are heavily influenced by random variation. After more observations accumulate, the running estimate becomes much more stable and begins to settle near the true value of 0.04. This is exactly the behavior predicted by the Law of Large Numbers.
The key takeaway is not that the estimate becomes perfect, but that larger samples make it more dependable. In practice, this is why A/B tests with very small sample sizes can be misleading, while larger experiments tend to produce more credible comparisons.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
A point estimate is useful, but it is not enough by itself. We also want to know how precise that estimate is. In other words, how much would the estimated difference change if the experiment were repeated with a different random sample of visitors?
The bootstrap provides a practical way to answer that question. Instead of relying entirely on a closed-form formula, I repeatedly resample from the observed data with replacement. For each bootstrap resample, I recompute the statistic of interest. The spread of those bootstrap estimates tells us how variable the estimator is, and the standard deviation of the bootstrap distribution gives an estimated standard error.

boot_thetas = []

for _ in range(1000):
    A_star = np.random.choice(A, size=n, replace=True)
    B_star = np.random.choice(B, size=n, replace=True)
    boot_thetas.append(A_star.mean() - B_star.mean())

boot_thetas = np.array(boot_thetas)
boot_se = boot_thetas.std(ddof=1)

piA_hat = A.mean()
piB_hat = B.mean()

analytical_se = np.sqrt(
    piA_hat * (1 - piA_hat) / n +
    piB_hat * (1 - piB_hat) / n
)

ci_lower = theta_hat - 1.96 * boot_se
ci_upper = theta_hat + 1.96 * boot_se

se_table = pd.DataFrame({
    "Quantity": [
        "Estimated Difference",
        "Bootstrap SE",
        "Analytical SE",
        "95% CI Lower",
        "95% CI Upper"
    ],
    "Value": [theta_hat, boot_se, analytical_se, ci_lower, ci_upper]
})

se_table["Value"] = se_table["Value"].round(4)
se_table

The bootstrap standard error and the analytical standard error are very similar, which is reassuring. That similarity suggests that the analytical formula is working well here, but it also shows that the bootstrap is able to recover the same level of uncertainty using only resampling.
Using the bootstrap standard error, I construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\theta\). This interval gives a range of values that are plausible for the true difference in sign-up rates. If the interval excludes zero, that is evidence that the two CTAs may genuinely differ in performance rather than appearing different only because of sampling noise.
This confidence interval provides a range of plausible values for the true treatment effect. If the interval does not include zero, it suggests that the difference in sign-up rates is unlikely to be due to random chance alone. ## The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) is one of the central ideas behind classical inference. It states that, under fairly general conditions, the sampling distribution of a sample mean becomes approximately Normal as the sample size increases. Since my estimator is a difference in sample means, the same idea applies here as well.
This result matters because it allows us to approximate the distribution of \(\hat{\theta}\) using a Normal distribution, which in turn makes confidence intervals and hypothesis tests possible.
To illustrate the CLT, I repeatedly simulate the estimator \(\hat{\theta}\) for four different sample sizes: \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\). For each sample size, I generate 1,000 separate estimates and examine the resulting histograms.

sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500]
sampling_results = {}

for n_s in sample_sizes:
    thetas = []
    for _ in range(1000):
        A_s = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n_s)
        B_s = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n_s)
        thetas.append(A_s.mean() - B_s.mean())
    sampling_results[n_s] = np.array(thetas)

pd.DataFrame({
    "Sample Size": sample_sizes,
    "Mean of Estimates": [sampling_results[s].mean() for s in sample_sizes],
    "SD of Estimates": [sampling_results[s].std(ddof=1) for s in sample_sizes]
}).round(4)

For small sample sizes, the distribution of \(\hat{\theta}\) appears rough and somewhat irregular. As the sample size increases, the histogram becomes smoother, more symmetric, and more bell-shaped. This is exactly what the Central Limit Theorem predicts.
The key point is that the underlying data are still Bernoulli, not Normal. What becomes approximately Normal is the sampling distribution of the estimator. That is what makes large-sample inference possible in this setting.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
Now that we have seen why \(\hat{\theta}\) behaves well in large samples, we can use that result to perform a formal hypothesis test. The null hypothesis is
\[
H_0: \theta = 0,
\]
which says that the two CTAs have the same true sign-up rate. The alternative hypothesis is
\[
H_1: \theta \neq 0,
\]
which says that the sign-up rates differ.
To test the null, I standardize the estimated treatment effect using its estimated standard error:
\[
z = \frac{\hat{\theta} - 0}{SE(\hat{\theta})}.
\]
The logic is straightforward. By the CLT, the estimator is approximately Normal in large samples. Under the null, its mean is zero. Dividing by its standard deviation therefore produces a statistic that is approximately standard Normal. Once we know the null distribution of the test statistic, we can compute a p-value.
This is often loosely described as a “t-test,” but in this Bernoulli setting the large-sample justification is more naturally a z-test. The distinction is not especially important here in practice, because with large samples the standard Normal and t distributions are extremely similar.

z_stat = theta_hat / analytical_se
p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z_stat)))

test_table = pd.DataFrame({
    "Statistic": ["Estimated Difference", "Standard Error", "z-Statistic", "p-Value"],
    "Value": [theta_hat, analytical_se, z_stat, p_value]
})

test_table["Value"] = test_table["Value"].round(4)
test_table

The test statistic measures how many standard errors the observed estimate lies away from zero. A large absolute value indicates that the observed difference would be unlikely if the null hypothesis were true.
The p-value summarizes that evidence. If it is small, we reject the null hypothesis and conclude that the data provide evidence of a difference between the two CTAs. If it is not small, we do not have strong enough evidence to rule out the possibility that the observed gap was produced by chance alone.
Under the null hypothesis, the standardized test statistic follows an approximate standard Normal distribution. This allows us to calculate a p-value, which represents the probability of observing a result at least as extreme as ours if the null hypothesis were true. ## The T-Test as a Regression
The same comparison can also be written as a simple linear regression. To do this, I stack the two groups into one dataset and define an indicator variable \(D_i\) that equals 1 if the visitor saw CTA A and 0 if the visitor saw CTA B. I then estimate
\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i.
\]
This regression has a very intuitive interpretation. When \(D_i = 0\), the expected value of \(Y_i\) is just the mean of group B, so \(\beta_0\) estimates \(\pi_B\). When \(D_i = 1\), the expected value is \(\beta_0 + \beta_1\), which estimates \(\pi_A\). That means \(\beta_1\) must equal the difference between the two group means, so it estimates \(\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\).

Y = np.concatenate([A, B])
D = np.concatenate([np.ones(len(A)), np.zeros(len(B))])

reg_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Y": Y,
    "D": D
})

model = sm.OLS(reg_df["Y"], sm.add_constant(reg_df["D"])).fit()

regression_table = pd.DataFrame({
    "Quantity": [
        "Intercept",
        "Treatment Coefficient",
        "Standard Error of Treatment",
        "t-Statistic",
        "p-Value"
    ],
    "Value": [
        model.params["const"],
        model.params["D"],
        model.bse["D"],
        model.tvalues["D"],
        model.pvalues["D"]
    ]
})

regression_table["Value"] = regression_table["Value"].round(4)

As expected, the coefficient on the treatment indicator is numerically the same as the difference in sample means computed earlier. The corresponding test statistic and p-value are also essentially the same, aside from minor differences that can arise from how the standard error is estimated.
This equivalence matters because it shows that a simple two-group A/B test is already a regression problem. Once the problem is written in regression form, it becomes easy to add control variables, interactions, or more complicated treatment structures while using the same basic inferential framework.
The coefficient on the treatment indicator matches the difference in sample means computed earlier, confirming the equivalence between the two approaches.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
Suppose an impatient manager does not want to wait until the experiment is complete. Instead, she checks the results after every 100 visitors per group and plans to stop the experiment as soon as one interim test is statistically significant at the 5% level.
This strategy is tempting, but it breaks the logic of a classical fixed-sample hypothesis test. A single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% Type I error rate under the null. But if we repeatedly test the same experiment as the data accumulate, each additional look creates another opportunity to reject by chance. The overall false positive rate can therefore become much larger than 5%.
To demonstrate this, I simulate a world in which the null hypothesis is true. I set \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\), so there is no real treatment effect. For each experiment, I simulate 1,000 observations per group, compute a p-value after every 100 observations, and record whether any of the 10 interim looks is significant. I then repeat the entire experiment 10,000 times.

def peeking_experiment():
    A_null = np.random.binomial(1, 0.20, 1000)
    B_null = np.random.binomial(1, 0.20, 1000)

    for k in range(100, 1001, 100):
        A_k = A_null[:k]
        B_k = B_null[:k]

        theta_k = A_k.mean() - B_k.mean()
        se_k = np.sqrt(
            A_k.mean() * (1 - A_k.mean()) / k +
            B_k.mean() * (1 - B_k.mean()) / k
        )

        if se_k == 0:
            continue

        z_k = theta_k / se_k
        p_k = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z_k)))

        if p_k < 0.05:
            return 1
    return 0

peeking_results = np.array([peeking_experiment() for _ in range(10000)])
false_positive_rate = peeking_results.mean()

peeking_table = pd.DataFrame({
    "Quantity": ["Nominal Type I Error", "Empirical Type I Error with Peeking"],
    "Value": [0.05, false_positive_rate]
})

peeking_table["Value"] = peeking_table["Value"].round(4)
peeking_table








Quantity
Value




0
Nominal Type I Error
0.0500


1
Empirical Type I Error with Peeking
0.1952







The simulation typically shows that the empirical false positive rate under repeated peeking is well above the nominal 5% level. In other words, even when there is truly no difference between the two CTAs, repeated interim testing makes it much easier to “discover” a winner by chance.
This is the practical danger of peeking. If analysts continuously monitor results and stop as soon as significance appears, they are no longer running a valid 5% hypothesis test. The reported p-value becomes misleading because the testing procedure itself has changed. In real A/B testing, this is why sequential testing methods or pre-registered stopping rules are so important.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MaxDiff (also called Best-Worst Scaling) is a survey methodology for measuring how strongly people prefer one item over another. On each screen of the survey, the respondent sees a small subset of items (here, 4) and selects the most-preferred and least-preferred item from that subset. They repeat this over many screens, building up a rich picture of preferences without asking people to rate anything on a numeric scale.
Why MaxDiff instead of a 1–10 rating scale? Humans are good at trade-offs — picking a best and a worst — but unreliable at calibrating numeric scales consistently. Some people always use the top of the scale; others spread their ratings uniformly regardless of true preferences. MaxDiff forces real differentiation between items and avoids scale-use bias entirely.
In this assignment, the 10 items are the 10 core MSBA classes at UCSD Rady. The goal is to estimate students’ relative preference for each class using three different methods, and to compare what each tells us:

A simple counts analysis — % times each class was picked best, minus % times picked worst
The multinomial logit (MNL) model fit by maximum likelihood using optim()
The same MNL model fit by Bayesian methods via Metropolis-Hastings MCMC

The three methods should broadly agree on the ranking of classes; they will differ in how they quantify preference strength and communicate uncertainty.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
Counts Analysis
The counts analysis is the simplest way to summarize MaxDiff data. For each item \(j\), we compute the percentage of times it was picked as best (out of the number of times it was shown), and the percentage of times it was picked as worst. The difference is a simple score:
\[
\text{score}_j \;=\; \%\text{best}_j \;-\; \%\text{worst}_j
\]
A score near +1 means an item is almost always picked as best when shown; near -1 means it is almost always picked as worst; near 0 means it is polarizing or middle-of-the-pack.


Code
counts <- choices |>
  group_by(item) |>
  summarise(
    times_shown = n(),
    times_best  = sum(response ==  1),
    times_worst = sum(response == -1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    pct_best  = times_best  / times_shown,
    pct_worst = times_worst / times_shown,
    score     = pct_best - pct_worst
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(score))
counts$item_label <- labels$item_label[match(counts$item, labels$item)]

counts |>
  mutate(across(c(pct_best, pct_worst, score), \(x) round(x, 3))) |>
  select(item, item_label, times_shown, times_best, times_worst,
         pct_best, pct_worst, score) |>
  kable(caption = "Counts analysis: ranked by score (% best − % worst)")

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
section: From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
text:
From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
Before fitting an MNL, we need to understand how the MaxDiff survey data converts into MNL choice observations.
The MNL model assigns each item \(j\) a utility coefficient \(\beta_j\). On a task showing items \(\{a, b, c, d\}\), the probability that item \(b\) is picked best is the soft-max:
\[
P(\text{best} = b) \;=\; \frac{\exp(\beta_b)}{\exp(\beta_a) + \exp(\beta_b) + \exp(\beta_c) + \exp(\beta_d)}
\]
The probability that item \(c\) is then picked worst — from the remaining 3 items, with utilities flipped — is:
\[
P(\text{worst} = c \mid \text{best} = b) \;=\; \frac{\exp(-\beta_c)}{\exp(-\beta_a) + \exp(-\beta_c) + \exp(-\beta_d)}
\]
So each task gives two MNL observations: the best-pick from the full set, and the worst-pick from the remaining set. The joint log-likelihood contribution of one task is \(\log P(\text{best}) + \log P(\text{worst} \mid \text{best})\).
Identifiability: the soft-max is invariant to adding a constant to every \(\beta\), so only \(J - 1 = 9\) of the 10 coefficients are identified. We fix \(\beta_1 = 0\) — item 1 as the reference class — and estimate \(\beta_2, \ldots, \beta_{10}\).


Code
tasks <- choices |>
  group_by(resp_id, task) |>
  reframe(
    items      = list(item),
    best_item  = item[response ==  1],
    worst_item = item[response == -1]
  ) |>
  distinct()

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Maximum Likelihood
The full log-likelihood, summing over all \(N \cdot T\) tasks, is:
\[
\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) \;=\; \sum_{\text{tasks}} \Big[\, \beta_{j^*_b} \;-\; \log \!\!\!\sum_{j \in \text{shown}}\!\!\! \exp(\beta_j) \;-\; \beta_{j^*_w} \;-\; \log \!\!\!\!\!\sum_{j \in \text{shown} \setminus \{j^*_b\}}\!\!\!\! \exp(-\beta_j) \,\Big]
\]
where \(j^*_b\) is the best-picked item and \(j^*_w\) is the worst-picked item in each task.


Code
ll <- function(beta_free, tasks_df) {
  beta  <- c(0, beta_free)
  total <- 0
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(tasks_df))) {
    shown  <- unlist(tasks_df$items[i])
    b_item <- tasks_df$best_item[i]
    w_item <- tasks_df$worst_item[i]
    v       <- beta[shown]
    ll_best <- beta[b_item] - (max(v) + log(sum(exp(v - max(v)))))
    remaining <- shown[shown != b_item]
    w         <- -beta[remaining]
    ll_worst  <- (-beta[w_item]) - (max(w) + log(sum(exp(w - max(w)))))
    total <- total + ll_best + ll_worst
  }
  total
}




Code

  par      = rep(0, 9),
  fn       = ll,
  tasks_df = tasks,
  method   = "BFGS",
  hessian  = TRUE,

)

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Bayesian Estimation
We fit the same MNL model using Bayesian inference. The posterior over \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) is:
\[
\pi(\boldsymbol{\beta} \mid \boldsymbol{y}) \;\propto\; L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) \cdot \pi(\boldsymbol{\beta})
\]
We use a weakly informative multivariate normal prior: \(\boldsymbol{\beta} \sim N(\boldsymbol{0},\; 10 \cdot I)\). With \(\text{SD} \approx 3.2\) on each free \(\beta\), this prior is diffuse enough not to constrain the posterior.


Code
log_post <- function(beta_free, tasks_df) {
  ll(beta_free, tasks_df) + sum(dnorm(beta_free, 0, sqrt(10), log = TRUE))
}

mh <- function(beta0, n_iter, step, tasks_df) {
  K      <- length(beta0)
  draws  <- matrix(NA, n_iter, K)
  beta   <- beta0
  lp     <- log_post(beta, tasks_df)
  accept <- 0
  for (t in 1:n_iter) {
    beta_star <- beta + rnorm(K, 0, step)
    lp_star   <- log_post(beta_star, tasks_df)
    if (log(runif(1)) < lp_star - lp) {
      beta   <- beta_star
      lp     <- lp_star
      accept <- accept + 1
    }
    draws[t, ] <- beta
  }
  list(draws = draws, accept_rate = accept / n_iter)
}

All three methods agree strongly at the extremes — MGTA 495 Marketing Analytics is consistently the most preferred class, and MGTA 444 Analytics Consulting is consistently the least preferred, across all three approaches. Disagreements appear in the middle tier, where items have similar underlying utilities and small sample fluctuations can flip adjacent rankings.
What does the MNL add over the counts analysis? The MNL models the choice process explicitly — every item that was shown but not picked contributes evidence that it has lower utility than the item that was picked. Counts ignores this information entirely. The MNL therefore makes more efficient use of the data, especially for mid-tier items whose counts scores can be distorted by which competitors they happened to be shown with.
What does Bayes add over MLE? The key advantage is easy uncertainty quantification on derived quantities. A 95% credible interval on a share of preference is trivial under Bayes: push every MCMC draw through the soft-max and take the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles. Doing this from MLE requires the delta method — a first-order approximation that is less reliable in the tails. Bayes also opens the door to hierarchical extensions that model preference heterogeneity across respondents.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
Discussion
Across all three methods, the analysis reveals a consistent and interpretable preference hierarchy among the 10 MSBA core classes at UCSD Rady. MGTA 495 Marketing Analytics ranks first by a substantial margin (counts score 0.493, MLE share 0.284), suggesting students strongly value applied, career-facing analytics coursework. MGTA 453 Business Analytics and MGTA 455 Customer Analytics follow in second and third place. At the bottom, MGTA 444 Analytics Consulting (counts score −0.292) and MGTA 451 Marketing/Finance/Operations rank as the least preferred — perhaps because students perceive them as less analytically distinctive or more obligatory in nature.
For what kind of question would you reach for each method? Counts is ideal for a quick executive summary — no modeling assumptions, easy to explain to any audience. MLE provides rigorous point estimates with classical standard errors, the right tool when a single number needs to be reported. Bayesian inference shines when uncertainty on derived quantities matters: shares of preference, pairwise probability statements, or market simulations are all trivial to compute from MCMC draws without relying on approximations like the delta method.
Several caveats apply. The survey was administered to a self-selected sample of current MSBA students; preferences may not generalize to prospective students, alumni, or future cohorts. Item presentation order, prior coursework, and current standing in the program may all affect responses in ways we cannot disentangle from the data.
If I had more time, I would extend the model to a hierarchical (mixed) MNL that treats each respondent’s \(\boldsymbol{\beta}_i\) as a draw from a shared multivariate normal population distribution. This would reveal preference heterogeneity across students — for example, whether students in different specialization tracks value different classes — and produce more accurate individual-level utility estimates.
